content:
The hidden cost of “user-friendly” tech, and what happens when you finally claim root access to your digital life.

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

🖥️ The Terminal Mindset: Series Introduction
For most users, computer usage is a process that involves clicking through multiple layers of carefully guarded glass provided by corporations. Users only click on the buttons provided and agree to the processes given to them. However, below this clean and shiny surface lies a powerful engine just waiting to be unleashed.
The Terminal Mindset is an unapologetic Sunday series focusing on the philosophy and power of Linux and open-source architecture. The purpose of this series is not to provide technical manuals. Instead, this is a statement of the philosophy of power users, developers, and security analysts who have no use being considered guests on their own computers.
📍 Series: The Terminal Mindset
Episode 1: Why I Stopped Renting My Operating System
I bought the hardware, but the brain still belonged to the corporations. Here is how I managed to escape their walled gardens.
First of all, here is one harsh reality that might enrage some Apple and Microsoft enthusiasts: when buying a laptop these days, you do not own it.
Indeed, you can have the brushed aluminum case, the silicon chips, the battery, and the glass screen as yours. However, unless you choose an open-source system, you will never have ownership of the brain behind that computer. It will belong to a mega-corporation, while you will be a mere tenant living in its digital premises.
You are using a licensed operating system that keeps an eye on your telemetry, makes mandatory reboots for updates even in the middle of your important tasks, and does not let you alter anything at your discretion. It might be the costly “golden handcuffs” of macOS or the bloated corridors full of ads of Windows 11.
The doors to the architecture underneath are bolted in order to ensure that everything is “safe and simple.” Yet what about when you choose not to be protected from your machine anymore?
Seeing the Matrix: Killing the “Boring”
The move to open source is an abrupt change in paradigm. In the walled garden model, when the manual process is tedious, redundant or cumbersome, you are forced to wait until a corporate developer decides to provide a paid solution to your problem. The system is opaque.
I hate tedious manual processes. In the world of Linux, the opaque black box is broken wide open.
Open-source software is more than just “free” in terms of cost; it is free as in freedom of expression. With the transparency of the code, there is always a guarantee that you will be aware of everything that your computer does all the time. Instead of purchasing a suite of software, you sit at your computer, create a shell script and create your own digital solution. You are not operating through a user interface anymore; you are communicating with the very DNA of the machine.
The Architect’s Canvas
The operating system will never dictate that you learn to work according to its system of operation. Instead, it must adapt to your workflow.
While proprietary operating systems force the user into whatever interface has been designed by the corporation for the average Joe, things take a completely different turn when it comes to the open-source community. The desktop becomes just an option.
Removing all those standard pre-designed components and creating a completely fresh and clean Kali Linux environment makes the interaction with computers drastically different. Putting together the custom-made KDE Plasma desktop environment by configuring each and every widget and keyboard shortcut exactly the way you need is turning your computer into a command center of efficiency.
Kernel-Level Sorcery (The Ultimate Flex)
The greatest thing about Linux is not making your desktop look amazing; it is the raw, horrifying ability to have root access to the kernel.
Due to full control in Linux, one is able to create and tweak something which would be considered forbidden magic in a proprietary operating system. Using the latest technologies like eBPF (Extended Berkeley Packet Filter), you will be able to connect directly to the nervous system of your PC.
Full control is exactly the reason why I started developing Aegis-LX — adaptive Linux security solution using eBPF and machine learning. Rather than installing some third-party antivirus which eats up your RAM for no reason at all, you can create your own framework and watch system calls in real time.
This is the greatest demonstration of a terminal mindset ever. You are not using an operating system in order to browse the internet; you are creating your own architecture in order to find the adversary.
The Takeaway
Removing the training wheels is scary. The command line doesn’t baby you, and if you accidentally type rm -rf /, it’ll be happy to allow you to break your system. But that is the cost of total freedom.

• Proprietary systems are products, but Linux is an architecture.

• The open-source model makes you learn what is going on inside your computer system, not just how to use it.

• Customization leads to perfection.
The next time that your computer demands an update or prevents you from deleting an unused default app, think about who really owns the machine.
When you have root privileges, you’ll never return to the walled garden.
Stay sharp, stay curious. — BreachHead
Debate time: Apple and Windows users, convince me I’m wrong. Tell me why your “walled garden” is better than root access. Linux users, back me up in the replies. Let’s see who brings the best arguments.
